pattern: Enumerating every starting index and extending until the divisible-by-p budget is spent, while hashing each window's contents, counts the distinct qualifying subarrays without double-counting repeats.
hints: Two subarrays count once only if their element sequences differ, so you need a way to deduplicate identical content.
From each start index, extend rightward and stop as soon as the count of elements divisible by p would exceed k.
Record each valid window in a set keyed by its element tuple (or via a trie/rolling hash); the set's size is the answer.

edge_inputs.description: p=1 makes every element divisible, all subarrays distinct here
k equals length so no window is rejected
budget zero-effectively forces single divisible element

stress.description: moderate array over a tiny value range to force heavy deduplication
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 200))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 5) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, n)
    p = rng.randint(1, 5)
    return [nums, k, p]
stress.note: small values create many identical windows to deduplicate
